g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Execution of a Complete Instruction. This is fundamental to understanding how CPUs work.
g.text: Module 8: Digital Design and Computer Organization

g.data-narration: An instruction is a command that tells the CPU to perform a specific task. It's the basic unit of computation.
g.text: An instruction is a set of operations that a computer's processor executes to perform
a specific task. It tells the CPU what to do with data.

g.data-narration: The instruction cycle has four main stages: Fetch, Decode, Execute, and Store. Let's explore each stage.
In the Fetch stage, the CPU retrieves the instruction from memory. It uses the program counter to know which instruction to fetch.
g.text: Retrieving the instruction from memory using the Program Counter (PC).

g.data-narration: In the Decode stage, the CPU interprets the binary instruction, extracting the opcode and operands to understand what operation to perform.
g.text: Converting binary instruction into machine-specific format. Extract opcode and operands.
Techniques: Branch Prediction, Out-of-order Execution, Speculative Execution

g.data-narration: In the Execute stage, the CPU performs the actual operation. The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) handles arithmetic and logical operations.
g.text: The ALU performs the operation specified by the decoded instruction.

g.data-narration: In the Store stage, the result is written back to memory or a register. This completes the instruction execution cycle.
g.text: Writing the result of execution to memory or registers.

g.data-narration: Modern CPUs use multiple execution stages: ID (Decode), EX (Execute), MD (Memory Access), and WB (Write Back).
g.text: Modern processors break execution into multiple pipeline stages for efficiency.

g.data-narration: There are three main types of instructions: Arithmetic, Load/Store, and Control Flow. Each type handles different operations.
Pipelining improves throughput by overlapping instruction execution. Multiple instructions are processed simultaneously in different pipeline stages.
g.text: Breaking instruction execution into stages to increase throughput.
Multiple instructions execute in parallel at different stages!

g.data-narration: Three key concepts enable high performance: ILP (parallel execution), out-of-order execution, and speculative execution.
The execution time formula helps calculate total time. The pipelining theorem shows how pipelining improves performance.
For quick revision, remember the four stages, understand pipelining benefits, and review instruction types and execution modes.
g.text: • Memorize the four stages: Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store
• Understand how pipelining improves CPU throughput
• Know the three instruction types: Arithmetic, Load/Store, Control Flow
• Remember the execution modes: ID, EX, MD, WB

g.data-narration: Thank you for learning about instruction execution. This fundamental concept is essential for understanding computer architecture and optimization.
g.text: Instruction execution is the core of CPU operation
Four stages: Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store
Pipelining enables parallel execution for better performance
Module 8: Digital Design and Computer Organization
